_meta.purpose: DP sub-classifier — identify which kind of DP applies and what the state/transition looks like
_meta.usage: Stage 3H — triggered when structural analysis confirms overlapping subproblems
_meta.howToUse: Work through stateIndexType first, then transitionType, then check optimizations

stateIndexTypes.name: 1D DP — single position index
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i] = answer for prefix ending at or including index i
recognize: Single sequence input
Answer at position i depends only on previous positions
No two-sequence interaction
No range or subset involved
typicalTransitions: dp[i] = max/min over all j < i of dp[j] + cost(j, i)
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[0] = 0 or dp[0] = a[0] depending on problem
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: If dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1] — reduce to two variables O(1) space
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Ensure dp[i] meaning is consistent — ending AT i vs ending BY i are different.

stateIndexTypes.name: 2D DP — two sequence indices
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i][j] = answer considering first i elements of s1 and first j elements of s2
recognize: Two string or sequence inputs
Problem involves matching, aligning, or comparing two sequences
Edit distance, LCS, or alignment problems
typicalTransitions: Else: dp[i][j] = max/min of dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1] + cost
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[0][j] = j (delete all of s2 prefix), dp[i][0] = i (delete all of s1 prefix)
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Only need previous row — reduce from O(nm) to O(m) space
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: At n=m=10^4 full table needs 400MB. Always apply rolling row optimization for large inputs.

stateIndexTypes.name: 2D DP — single sequence, two pointers (interval DP)
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i][j] = answer for subarray/substring from index i to j inclusive
recognize: Single sequence but answer involves ranges within it
Optimal way to split or combine a range
Problem involves palindromes, matrix chain, or burst balloons style problems
Merging adjacent elements optimally
typicalTransitions: dp[i][j] = min/max over all k in [i, j-1] of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(i, j, k)
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[i][i] = 0 or base value, dp[i][i+1] = cost of adjacent pair
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Cannot easily optimize — O(n^2) space is typical
stateIndexTypes.fillOrder: CRITICAL — must fill by increasing interval length, not by row
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Fill order matters critically. Fill dp[i][j] for length 1, then length 2, ..., then length n. Wrong fill order gives wrong answers.

stateIndexTypes.name: Bitmask DP — subset state
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[mask] or dp[mask][i] = answer for subset represented by mask, currently at element i
recognize: n is small — typically n <= 20
Problem involves assigning each of n items optimally
All subsets need to be considered
TSP-type problems, assignment problems
typicalTransitions: dp[mask | (1<<j)][j] = dp[mask][i] + cost(i, j) for all j not in mask
dp[mask] = min over all i in mask of dp[mask ^ (1<<i)] + cost
examples: Minimum cost to assign tasks to workers
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: 2^20 * 20 = 20M states is fine. 2^25 = 33M states starts being risky. Never use for n > 20.

stateIndexTypes.name: Tree DP — node and subtree state
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[node][state] = answer for subtree rooted at node with given state
recognize: Input is explicitly a tree
Answer for a node depends on answers for its children
Problem involves subtree properties or path properties
Rooted tree traversal
typicalTransitions: dp[node][0] = not selecting node, dp[node][1] = selecting node
For paths: track best single path going down, update global answer at each node
examples: Number of nodes in largest subtree satisfying property
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Recursive DFS may stack overflow for n=10^5 with skewed tree. Use iterative post-order.

stateIndexTypes.name: Digit DP — position and tight constraint
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[pos][tight][accumulated_state] = count of valid numbers from position pos onward
recognize: Count numbers in range [0, N] satisfying digit property
N can be very large — up to 10^18
Property depends on individual digits
Digit sum, digit count, digit pattern constraints
typicalTransitions: For each digit d from 0 to (tight ? current_digit : 9):
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Memoize — states are (pos, tight, accumulated). tight is boolean so 2 values.
examples: Count numbers with digit sum = k up to N
Count numbers with no two adjacent equal digits
Count stepping numbers (adjacent digits differ by 1)
Count numbers divisible by d with digit constraints
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: tight must be passed correctly through recursion. Leading zero handling needs separate flag.

stateIndexTypes.name: 0/1 Knapsack — item and capacity
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with weight capacity w
recognize: Each item used at most once
Fixed capacity or budget constraint
Maximize value subject to weight constraint
Binary choice per item — include or exclude
typicalTransitions: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w - weight[i]] + value[i]) if w >= weight[i]
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Iterate w from W down to weight[i] — reduces to O(W) space with 1D array
stateIndexTypes.fillOrder: For space optimization — iterate items outer, weights inner in DECREASING order
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Decreasing order for 0/1 (each item once). Increasing order for unbounded (item reusable). Mixing these is the most common knapsack bug.

stateIndexTypes.name: Unbounded Knapsack — item reusable
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[w] = max value achievable with capacity exactly w (items reusable)
recognize: Each item can be used unlimited times
Coin change type problems
Rod cutting — cut rod into pieces, maximize value
typicalTransitions: dp[w] = max over all items i where weight[i] <= w of dp[w - weight[i]] + value[i]
stateIndexTypes.fillOrder: Iterate w from 1 to W (increasing) — allows reuse of same item
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Increasing order for unbounded. The only difference from 0/1 knapsack is fill direction.

stateIndexTypes.name: LIS-type DP — patience sorting / binary search optimization
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i] = length of longest increasing subsequence ending at index i
recognize: Looking for longest subsequence with ordering property
Elements must be in strictly or non-strictly increasing order
Can extend subsequence by appending elements
typicalTransitions: O(n^2): dp[i] = max over all j < i where a[j] < a[i] of dp[j] + 1
O(n log n): Maintain tails array — binary search for insertion position
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[i] = 1 for all i (each element alone is subsequence of length 1)
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: O(n log n) approach uses O(n) tails array
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: At n=10^5 must use O(n log n) patience sorting. O(n^2) will TLE.

stateIndexTypes.name: State Machine DP — explicit states with transitions
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[i][state] = answer at index i being in given state
recognize: Problem has distinct modes or phases
Can transition between modes at each step
Stock trading problems with cooldown or transaction limits
Problems with at most k operations allowed
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[0][each_state] = initial value for that state
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Only need previous row — O(states) space
examples: Best time to buy and sell stock with cooldown
Best time to buy and sell with at most k transactions
Paint fence with at most 2 consecutive same color
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: Draw state transition diagram explicitly before coding. Missing a transition is the most common bug.

stateIndexTypes.name: Grid DP — row and column indices
stateIndexTypes.stateRepresents: dp[r][c] = answer for reaching or considering cell (r, c)
recognize: 2D grid input
Movement restricted to specific directions (usually right and down)
Counting paths or finding optimal path in grid
No backtracking — movement is one-directional
stateIndexTypes.baseCase: dp[0][c] = prefix values along top row, dp[r][0] = prefix values along left column
stateIndexTypes.spaceOptimization: Only need previous row — O(cols) space
examples: Dungeon game (min health to reach end)
stateIndexTypes.watchOut: If movement is allowed in all 4 directions — grid DP breaks, must use BFS/DFS instead.

transitionTypes.name: Adjacent transition
transitionTypes.pattern: dp[i] depends only on dp[i-1] or dp[i-2]
transitionTypes.spaceOptimization: O(1) — just keep last 1 or 2 values

transitionTypes.name: All previous transition
transitionTypes.pattern: dp[i] = max/min over all j < i of dp[j] + f(j, i)
transitionTypes.spaceOptimization: Cannot reduce below O(n) without special structure
transitionTypes.optimization: If f(j, i) has special structure — may apply Convex Hull Trick or Divide and Conquer DP

transitionTypes.name: Range split transition
transitionTypes.pattern: dp[i][j] = min/max over all k in [i, j) of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost
transitionTypes.optimization: Knuth optimization if cost satisfies quadrangle inequality — reduces O(n^3) to O(n^2)

transitionTypes.name: Push vs Pull DP
transitionTypes.push: From dp[i] update future states: dp[j] = f(dp[i]) for all valid j > i
transitionTypes.pull: dp[i] = f(dp[j]) for all valid j that can reach i
transitionTypes.note: Both are equivalent. Pull is more common in top-down. Push is natural in BFS-style DP.

dpOptimizations.name: Convex Hull Trick (CHT)
appliesWhen: dp[i] = min over j < i of dp[j] + b[j] * a[i]
Transition is of form: linear function in a[i] with slope b[j] and intercept dp[j]
Standard O(n^2) transition needs to be reduced
dpOptimizations.reduces: O(n^2) → O(n log n) or O(n) if slopes are monotonic
dpOptimizations.howItWorks: Maintain lower convex hull of lines y = b[j]*x + dp[j]. Query minimum at x = a[i].
examples: Minimum cost to divide array with penalty
Certain DP on sequences with linear cost

dpOptimizations.name: Divide and Conquer DP Optimization
appliesWhen: dp[i][j] = min over k in [i, j] of dp[i-1][k] + cost(k, j)
Optimal split point opt(i, j) is monotone: opt(i, j) <= opt(i, j+1)
Cost function satisfies the quadrangle inequality
dpOptimizations.reduces: O(n^2) per DP layer → O(n log n) per layer
dpOptimizations.howItWorks: Recursively solve left and right halves, sharing the optimal split point search.

dpOptimizations.name: Knuth Optimization
appliesWhen: Interval DP: dp[i][j] = min over k of dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost(i, j)
Cost satisfies: cost(a, c) + cost(b, d) <= cost(a, d) + cost(b, c) for a<=b<=c<=d
examples: Matrix chain with specific cost structure

dpOptimizations.name: Rolling Array Space Optimization
appliesWhen: dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1] (or small constant number of previous rows)
Do not need to reconstruct path — just need final value
dpOptimizations.howItWorks: Use two 1D arrays — current and previous. Or use dp[i % 2] indexing.
dpOptimizations.watchOut: Cannot use rolling array if you need to reconstruct the actual path — need full table for backtracking.

dpOptimizations.name: Monotonic Queue DP Optimization
appliesWhen: dp[i] = min/max over j in sliding window [i-k, i-1] of dp[j] + cost
Window of valid previous states has fixed or bounded size
dpOptimizations.howItWorks: Maintain deque of indices in monotonic order. Front is always the optimal previous state.
examples: Jump game with at most k steps

completenessCheck.question: Can I reconstruct the full solution from just the state?

example.state: dp[i] = max sum ending at index i
example.check: Can I find global max? YES — take max over all dp[i]

failureExample.state: dp[i] = true if sum of some subset equals i
failureExample.check: Can I find which elements were chosen? NO — need to track choices
failureExample.fix: Add parent tracking array or reconstruct by tracing back

redundancyCheck.question: Is any part of the state derivable from other parts?

example.check: Sum is computable from i and j — redundant dimension
example.fix: Reduce to dp[i][j] — saves O(sum_range) factor

dimensionReductionCheck.question: Do I actually need all dimensions or can one be eliminated?

example.state: dp[i][j] where j = previous element chosen
example.check: Is j always i-1 or i-2? If yes — eliminate j dimension
example.fix: dp[i] with implicit previous from loop structure

commonMistakes.mistake: Wrong fill order in interval DP
commonMistakes.description: Filling dp[i][j] by row (i from 0 to n) instead of by length (length from 1 to n)
commonMistakes.consequence: dp[i][k] or dp[k+1][j] not yet computed when needed
commonMistakes.fix: Always fill interval DP by increasing interval length: for len in range(1, n+1): for i in range(n-len+1): j = i + len - 1

commonMistakes.description: Iterating capacity forward (increasing) in 0/1 knapsack allows item reuse
commonMistakes.fix: 0/1: iterate capacity DECREASING. Unbounded: iterate capacity INCREASING

commonMistakes.mistake: State does not capture enough information
commonMistakes.description: dp[i] represents answer at position i but same position can be reached in different modes
commonMistakes.consequence: Different modes overwrite each other — wrong answer
commonMistakes.fix: Add state dimension for mode: dp[i][mode]

commonMistakes.description: Initializing dp[0] = 0 when it should be dp[0] = a[0] or vice versa
commonMistakes.fix: Carefully define what dp[0] means and initialize accordingly. Test on n=1.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgot to track parent pointers for reconstruction
commonMistakes.description: Computed optimal value but cannot trace back the actual solution
commonMistakes.consequence: Can output value but not the actual sequence or path
commonMistakes.fix: Maintain parent[] or choice[] array alongside dp[]. Fill during DP, trace back at end.
